home.title: Accurate Period Calculator
home.subtitle: Use our free Period Calculator to predict your next cycle, ovulation, and fertile window. No login required—100% private, secure, and accurate medical-grade tool. Our Period Calculator helps you track your menstrual cycle with precision.

about.title: About Period Calculator
about.subtitle: Empowering women with privacy-first health tools

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.description: We believe every woman deserves access to accurate, private menstrual health tools. Our mission is to provide a reliable period calculator that respects your privacy while helping you understand your body better.

whyWeBuilt.title: Why We Built This
whyWeBuilt.description: Many existing period tracking apps require you to create an account, share personal data with third parties, or worry about data breaches. We wanted to create something different.

items.privacyFirst: Privacy First - All your data stays on your device. We never see or store your personal information.
items.noAccount: No Account Required - Start using immediately without registration or login.
items.accurate: Accurate Predictions - Our algorithms are based on established medical research.
items.free: Completely Free - No premium features, no subscriptions, no hidden costs.

approach.title: Our Approach
approach.description: We are committed to providing accurate, evidence-based information. All our content is:

items.reviewed: Reviewed by professionals - Our content is reviewed by qualified healthcare professionals.
items.sources: Based on authoritative sources - We reference ACOG, NHS, WHO, and other authoritative medical organizations.
items.updated: Regularly updated - We review and update our content quarterly to ensure accuracy.

disclaimer.title: Important Disclaimer
disclaimer.description: Period Calculator is designed for informational purposes only and is not intended as a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always seek the advice of your physician or other qualified health provider with any questions you may have regarding a medical condition.

contact.title: Get in Touch
contact.description: Have questions or feedback? We'd love to hear from you.

footer.trustText: Your data never leaves your device.

calculator.subtitle: Track your cycle and predict your next period

privacyPolicy.title: Privacy Policy
privacyPolicy.intro: Your privacy is important to us. This policy explains how we collect, use, and protect your personal information when you use Period Calculator.

dataCollection.title: Data We Collect
dataCollection.description: Period Calculator is designed with privacy as our top priority. When you use our service, we collect minimal data necessary for functionality:
dataCollection.item0: First day of your last period (for cycle calculation)

dataStorage.title: How We Store Your Data
dataStorage.description: All your data is stored locally on your device using browser storage. We never upload your personal data to any server.
dataStorage.item0: Data is stored in your browser's localStorage
dataStorage.item2: You can delete your data at any time by clearing browser cache

dataUsage.title: How We Use Your Data
dataUsage.description: Your data is used exclusively to provide our core services:
dataUsage.item1: Predict your fertile window and ovulation day
dataUsage.item2: Generate calendar events for your predictions

cookies.title: Cookies
cookies.description: We use minimal cookies solely for essential functionality:
cookies.item2: These are functional cookies, not tracking cookies

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Services
thirdParty.description: We integrate with trusted third-party services to enhance your experience:
thirdParty.item0: Google Calendar (for adding events) - data is sent directly from your device to Google
thirdParty.item1: No data is shared with advertising networks

userRights.title: Your Rights
userRights.description: You have complete control over your data:
userRights.item0: Access your data at any time
userRights.item1: Delete your data instantly (clear browser cache)
userRights.item2: Export your data (not applicable as data stays on your device)
userRights.item3: Opt-out by simply not using the app

cta.title: Questions?
cta.description: We're here to help with any privacy concerns.

editorialPolicy.title: Editorial Policy
editorialPolicy.intro: Our editorial policy ensures that all health information provided on Period Calculator is accurate, up-to-date, and based on credible scientific sources. We are committed to transparency in our content creation process.

medicalSources.title: Medical Sources
medicalSources.description: We reference only authoritative medical organizations and peer-reviewed research to ensure our content meets the highest standards of accuracy:
medicalSources.item0: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) - Practice Bulletins
medicalSources.item1: World Health Organization (WHO) - Reproductive Health Guidelines
medicalSources.item2: National Health Service (NHS) UK - Menstrual Health Information
medicalSources.item4: National Institutes of Health (NIH) - PubMed Research

reviewProcess.title: Content Review Process
reviewProcess.description: Every piece of content goes through a rigorous review process to ensure accuracy and relevance:
reviewProcess.item0: Initial research by qualified health writers
reviewProcess.item2: Regular updates based on latest research

updateFrequency.title: Update Frequency
updateFrequency.description: We maintain our content to reflect the latest medical guidelines and research:
updateFrequency.item1: Medical guidelines updated as new research emerges

accuracyCommitment.title: Accuracy Commitment
accuracyCommitment.description: We strive for precision in all our health information while acknowledging the limitations of predictive tools:
accuracyCommitment.item0: Algorithms based on established medical research

disclaimer.title: Disclaimer
disclaimer.description: Important information about the limitations of our service:
disclaimer.item0: This is a prediction tool, not a medical device
disclaimer.item1: Results are estimates based on averages
disclaimer.item2: Consult healthcare professionals for medical advice
disclaimer.item3: We are not responsible for decisions made based on predictions

howToCalculate.title: How to Use the Period Calculator
howToCalculate.description: Learn how to use our free Period Calculator to track your menstrual cycle and predict your next period with accuracy.

step1.title: Enter Your Last Period Date
step1.description: Input the first day of your most recent period in the Period Calculator. This is the starting point for all cycle calculations.

step2.title: Set Your Cycle Length
step2.description: Enter your average cycle length (typically 21-35 days) in the Period Calculator. Our algorithm uses this to predict your next period accurately.

step3.title: Add Period Duration
step3.description: Specify how long your period typically lasts (usually 3-7 days) in the Period Calculator. This helps predict the menstruation phase.

step4.title: Get Your Predictions
step4.description: The Period Calculator calculates your next period, fertile window, ovulation day, and PMS dates based on medical research.

irregularHowToCalculate.title: How to Use the Irregular Period Calculator
irregularHowToCalculate.description: Learn how to use our Irregular Period Calculator to track uneven cycles and get accurate predictions based on your historical data.

step1.title: Enter Your Cycle History
step1.description: Input the start dates of your last 3-6 menstrual cycles. The more history you provide, the more accurate your predictions will be.

step2.title: Set Your Period Length
step2.description: Enter how many days your period typically lasts (usually 3-7 days). This helps calculate the exact dates of your next period.

step3.title: View Your Prediction Window
step3.description: See your predicted window with earliest, most likely, and latest dates. The visualization shows the range of possible start dates.

step4.title: Check Your Confidence Level
step4.description: Review your prediction confidence level based on cycle consistency. High confidence means your cycles are regular.

deepKnowledge.title: Understanding Your Monthly Rhythm
deepKnowledge.description: Most people think of their cycle as just the days they are bleeding, but your body is actually moving through a sophisticated, three-part biological journey every month. Understanding these shifts is exactly why a precise Period Calculator is about more than just 'day one'—it's about mapping your unique hormonal landscape. Our Period Calculator helps you track every phase of your menstrual cycle with precision.

follicularPhase.title: The Follicular Phase: The Buildup
follicularPhase.description: The Follicular Phase begins on the very first day of your period and lasts until you ovulate. During this time, your body is busy maturing an egg for release. As your estrogen levels gradually climb, you might notice a steady boost in your energy, mood, and even skin clarity. By tracking the start of this phase in our Period Calculator, the algorithm begins to learn the specific 'tempo' of your body's preparation. The Period Calculator tracks this phase to provide accurate predictions.

ovulation.title: Ovulation: The Main Event
ovulation.description: Ovulation is the brief but vital window—usually lasting only 12 to 24 hours—when an egg is released from the ovary. This is your peak fertility window. Because factors like stress, travel, or sleep can shift your ovulation date, relying on a simple average isn't always enough. Our Period Calculator analyzes your historical data to help you pinpoint this window, whether you are trying to conceive or simply want to stay in tune with your body's signals.

lutealPhase.title: The Luteal Phase: The Countdown
lutealPhase.description: After ovulation, you enter the Luteal Phase. This is the 10-to-16-day stretch where progesterone takes center stage, thickening the uterine lining. If you've ever wondered why you feel more sensitive or bloated a week before your period, this phase is the reason. A consistent Period Calculator helps you predict exactly when this phase begins, so you're never caught off guard by PMS or the start of your next cycle.

irregularDeepKnowledge.title: Understanding Irregular Periods: What It Means for Your Health
irregularDeepKnowledge.description: For many, the "perfect" 28-day cycle is more of a textbook myth than a biological reality. If your cycle arrives early, late, or skips a month entirely, you aren't alone. However, navigating this unpredictability requires more than just guesswork; it requires a specialized Irregular Period Calculator that understands the nuances of hormonal fluctuations.

normal.title: What Is Actually "Normal"?
normal.description: According to the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG), a typical adult menstrual cycle lasts anywhere from 21 to 35 days. Irregularity occurs when your cycle consistently falls outside this range or varies significantly in length from month to month. This is where a standard tracker fails and a dedicated Irregular Period Calculator becomes essential. By using an Irregular Period Calculator, you can move away from fixed dates and start predicting "windows" of time, which offers a much more realistic view of your reproductive health.

culprits.title: Common Culprits Behind the Chaos
culprits.description:
Why does your body deviate from the script? There are several biological and lifestyle factors that an Irregular Period Calculator helps you monitor:

1. Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS): A common hormonal imbalance that often causes skipped or prolonged cycles. An Irregular Period Calculator is often the first tool women use to realize their patterns need medical attention.

2. Thyroid Dysfunction: Your thyroid regulates your metabolism and affects your ovaries. If your thyroid is overactive or underactive, your data in an Irregular Period Calculator will likely show significant gaps.

3. Chronic Stress: High cortisol levels can suppress ovulation. By tracking these "stress gaps" in an Irregular Period Calculator, you can see the direct impact of your lifestyle on your cycle.

4. Perimenopause: As you approach menopause, cycles naturally become erratic. An Irregular Period Calculator helps you document this transition for your doctor.

calculator.title: How an Irregular Period Calculator Bridges the Gap
calculator.description: Unlike basic tools, a robust Irregular Period Calculator uses moving averages and standard deviations. When you input your history into an Irregular Period Calculator, it doesn't just add 28 days to your last start date. Instead, the Irregular Period Calculator analyzes the variance. If you are trying to conceive or simply want to be prepared, the Irregular Period Calculator provides a "likely window" for your next period. This data-driven approach used by an Irregular Period Calculator reduces the anxiety of the unknown, giving you a sense of control over your body.

doctor.title: When to Step Away from the Calculator and See a Doctor
doctor.description:
While an Irregular Period Calculator is an incredible tool for self-awareness, it is not a diagnostic device. Medical experts suggest scheduling an appointment if:

1. Your cycles consistently last longer than 35 days or shorter than 21 days.
2. You experience sudden, extreme irregularity after years of being regular.
3. Your period lasts longer than 7 days or is exceptionally heavy.

When you do visit a professional, the data logged in your Irregular Period Calculator will be your most valuable asset. It provides your physician with a clear, objective history of your symptoms and timing, making an accurate diagnosis much faster.

empowerment.title: Empowerment Through Data
empowerment.description: Your cycle is a vital sign, much like your blood pressure or heart rate. Trusting an Irregular Period Calculator to map your unique rhythm is the first step toward proactive health management. Whether you're dealing with PCOS or just a high-stress month, let our Irregular Period Calculator do the heavy lifting of prediction so you can focus on feeling your best.

latePeriod.question: Am I late for my period?
latePeriod.answer: A period is generally considered "late" if it hasn't arrived within five days of your expected start date, according to the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG). However, since few people have a perfectly rhythmic 28-day cycle, it's normal to see slight fluctuations of 2-7 days. If you are sexually active and your period is even one day late, the most reliable next step is a pregnancy test, as our Period Calculator provides an estimate based on averages, not biological certainty. Source: ACOG Guidelines.

nextPeriodDate.question: How do I calculate my next period date?
nextPeriodDate.answer: To find your next start date, you first need to identify "Day 1" of your last cycle. This is the very first day you noticed a steady flow. From there, add your average cycle length, which is the number of days from the start of one period to the start of the next. For example, if your last period began on March 1 and your average cycle is 28 days, your next period is predicted to start on March 29. According to NHS UK, most menstrual cycles range from 21 to 35 days.

safePeriod.question: How do I calculate my "safe period"?
safePeriod.answer: The "safe period" refers to the days in your cycle when you are least likely to conceive, typically at the very beginning and very end of your cycle. To calculate this, you must first identify your ovulation window, which is usually 12 to 16 days before your next period, according to ACOG. However, it is important to remember that the "Calendar Method" is not 100% accurate. Sperm can survive inside the body for up to five days, making it risky to rely on a calculator alone for contraception. Source: ACOG and NHS UK.

periodEarly.question: Why did my period come early this month?
periodEarly.answer: It is quite common for a period to arrive a few days early. According to research published in Women's Health Issues, this usually happens because ovulation occurred sooner than usual. Common triggers for an early cycle include high levels of stress (35% of cases), significant changes in your sleep schedule, intense new exercise routines, or even recent travel. If your period is consistently early (more than 7 days) or accompanied by unusual pain, consulting a healthcare provider is recommended.

normalFlow.question: How much flow is normal during a period?
normalFlow.answer: The average person loses between 30 ml and 80 ml of blood over the course of their entire period, about 2 to 5 tablespoons, according to ACOG. In practical terms, this means soaking through a pad or tampon every 3 to 4 hours. If you find yourself needing to change your protection every hour or noticing clots larger than a quarter, your flow may be considered heavy (menorrhagia), and you should seek medical advice. Source: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists.

pregnancyFromLmp.question: Why is pregnancy calculated from the last period (LMP)?
pregnancyFromLmp.answer: Because most people don't know the exact moment of conception, medical professionals use the Last Menstrual Period (LMP) as a clear, objective starting point, as recommended by ACOG and NHS. Since ovulation usually happens about two weeks after your period starts, this means that during the first two weeks of a "40-week pregnancy," you are not actually pregnant yet. This standard helps doctors more accurately estimate your due date and track fetal development milestones. Source: ACOG Practice Bulletin.

getPregnant.question: Will irregular periods affect my ability to get pregnant?
getPregnant.answer: While having an unpredictable cycle can make timing conception more challenging, it does not necessarily mean you cannot get pregnant. The primary hurdle is identifying your fertile window. Because ovulation may occur at different times each month, using an Irregular Period Calculator is vital to mapping out the range of days when you are most likely to conceive. From a clinical perspective, if you are not ovulating at all (anovulation), you should consult a specialist. However, for most, an Irregular Period Calculator provides the necessary data to narrow down the best days for intercourse, significantly increasing your chances of success.

dueDate.question: How do I calculate my due date with irregular periods?
dueDate.answer: Standard pregnancy math relies on the Naegele's Rule, which assumes a 28-day cycle. For those with variance, this method is often inaccurate. Medical professionals typically recommend an early dating ultrasound to confirm the gestational age. In the meantime, you can provide your doctor with the historical data from your Irregular Period Calculator. By showing the variance in your past cycles via an Irregular Period Calculator, you help your care team understand that your Day 1 might not align with standard averages, leading to a much more personalized and accurate estimated due date (EDD).

ovulation.question: How can I calculate ovulation if my cycles are irregular?
ovulation.answer: Calculating ovulation when your dates shift requires looking for a window rather than a single day. You should monitor physical signs like cervical mucus changes alongside the data provided by your Irregular Period Calculator. An advanced Irregular Period Calculator uses your previous 3 to 6 months of data to determine your shortest and longest cycles. By establishing this range, the Irregular Period Calculator can forecast a 5-to-7-day fertile window. Relying on this data-driven approach from an Irregular Period Calculator is much more effective than using a generic calendar that assumes everyone ovulates on Day 14.

averageCycle.question: How do I calculate my average cycle length with irregular periods?
averageCycle.answer: To find your average, you should sum the total days of your last six cycles and divide by six. However, for those with high variance, the simple average can be misleading. This is where an Irregular Period Calculator becomes indispensable. An Irregular Period Calculator doesn't just give you a flat average; it calculates the standard deviation to show you how much your cycle typically fluctuates. Understanding this variance through an Irregular Period Calculator helps you distinguish between a cycle that is slightly off and one that is truly abnormal, which is a key distinction in reproductive health.

pcos.question: Is an Irregular Period Calculator accurate for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)?
pcos.answer: For individuals with PCOS, hormones often don't follow a predictable rise and fall, making exact predictions difficult. While no digital tool can be 100% accurate for PCOS, an Irregular Period Calculator is an essential management tool. By consistently logging your data into an Irregular Period Calculator, you can identify emerging patterns that might otherwise go unnoticed. Most endocrinologists recommend using an Irregular Period Calculator to track these long-term trends, as this objective history is far more useful for medical diagnosis than relying on memory alone during a consultation.

inaccurate.question: What should I do if my predictions are inaccurate for three consecutive cycles?
inaccurate.answer: If your actual start dates consistently fall outside the window predicted by your Irregular Period Calculator, it may be a sign of an underlying hormonal shift or significant lifestyle stress. Medical experts at the Mayo Clinic suggest that cycle tracking as a vital sign is crucial. If the Irregular Period Calculator shows a persistent trend of increasing irregularity over 90 days, you should schedule an appointment with a gynecologist. Bring the digital logs from your Irregular Period Calculator to your appointment; this allows your doctor to see exactly how your cycle has deviated, facilitating a much faster and more accurate medical evaluation.
